Historical Monument · Maharashtra, India

Raigad Fort

The mountain throne of Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj

↓ scroll to explore
Location
Raigad District, Maharashtra
Elevation
820 m above sea level
Built / Expanded
c. 1656–1674 CE
Capital of
Maratha Empire

What Is Raigad Fort?

Raigad Fort, also known as Rairi in ancient records, is a magnificent hill fort perched atop an isolated mesa in the Sahyadri mountain range of Maharashtra, India. Rising 820 metres above sea level, its single steep access path made it virtually impregnable in medieval warfare. Today it stands as one of the most iconic symbols of Maratha pride and independence.

Originally built by the Chandrarao More clan in the early 13th century, Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj captured and completely transformed it in 1656 CE, expanding it into a grand capital with a royal palace, market streets, a durbar hall, water tanks, and residences for thousands — all on top of a mountain.

रायगड एक स्वर्ग — Raigad, a paradise on earth

✦ ✦ ✦

Location & History

The fort is located in the Mahad taluka of Raigad District, approximately 25 km from Mahad town. Deep in the Western Ghats, sheer cliffs drop hundreds of metres on all sides, making it a near-perfect natural fortress with commanding views of the surrounding valleys.

After seizing the fort from the Mores, Shivaji Maharaj invested enormous resources rebuilding it over two decades. On 6 June 1674 CE, in a grand coronation ceremony, Shivaji was crowned Chhatrapati (Emperor) at Raigad — announcing the birth of the sovereign Maratha Empire to the entire subcontinent.

Rajya Abhishek Sohala — Grand coronation anniversary celebration at Raigad Fort

Maha Darwaza — The legendary Grand Gate of Raigad Fort

"Raigad was not merely a fort — it was a statement. A kingdom built on a mountain, by a man who refused to bow."

— Historian's reflection on the Maratha Empire
✦ ✦ ✦

Historical Importance

Raigad served as the capital of the Maratha Empire for over a decade, the political, cultural, and administrative heart of a power that challenged both the Mughal Empire and European colonial forces. Shivaji's coronation here was a landmark moment — the revival of an independent Indian kingdom after centuries of foreign domination.

The fort is also the final resting place of Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj, who passed away here in 1680 CE. His samadhi (memorial tomb) within the fort remains a deeply revered pilgrimage site, making Raigad a symbol of cultural identity, self-governance, and the indomitable Maratha spirit.

Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj — Founder of the Maratha Empire

✦ ✦ ✦

Interesting Facts

  • The fort has over 1,737 rock-cut steps to the main entrance — the climb takes about 45 minutes on foot.
  • A modern ropeway (cable car) spans 750 m, carrying visitors to the top in just 4 minutes.
  • At its peak, Raigad had nearly 300 homes and a bazaar with 27 shops — a full city on a mountaintop.
  • The Jagdishwar Temple inside is dedicated to Lord Shiva and was Shivaji Maharaj's personal place of worship.
  • The Maha Darwaza (Grand Gate) has twin bastions each approximately 20–21 metres tall on either side.
  • Takmak Tok is a cliff inside the fort from which condemned prisoners were historically thrown — now fenced off.
  • Raigad was partially destroyed by Mughal emperor Aurangzeb's forces in 1689 but was never fully conquered.
  • The Archaeological Survey of India has declared it a protected monument of national importance.
  • Every year on Shivaji Jayanti, thousands of people trek the fort to honour the great Maratha king.
  • The Rajya Abhishek Sohala (coronation anniversary) is celebrated every year at Raigad with great enthusiasm and devotion.

Raigad Fort  ·  Maharashtra, India  ·  Protected Monument of National Importance
